text: During a thoracoscopic right lower lobectomy, the surgeon identifies and injures the anomalous artery within the inferior pulmonary ligament using an electrical hook. Curved color forceps are applied to control bleeding, followed by the placement of a titanium clip to clear space for the endostapler. The vascular bundle is then transected with the endostapler.

During the thoracoscopic rhizopalobectomy for pulmonary adenocarcinoma, the truncus anterior is injured by the electrocoagulation hook. Bleeding is controlled by site compression with endoscopic suction, followed by the introduction of curved scallop forceps to clamp the injury. The superior pulmonary vein is transected for better exposure, and the injured truncus anterior is sutured with side compression using suction. Once bleeding is controlled, the truncus anterior is further dissected and transected using an endostapler.

The surgeon retracts a lymph node tightly adhered to the superior vena cava using a stitch, then performs sharp dissection with scissors. A five-millimeter cut is accidentally made on the superior vena cava wall, which is compressed with a suction tip for wound evaluation. A non-needle holder is used to perform angiography with a five-zero pulling stitch. Intraoperative pathological assessment of other dissected lymph nodes shows no metastasis, leading to part of the node being left undissected.

During the thoracoscopic red epilobectomy, the surgeon addresses an injury to the exogenous vein caused by the electrocoagulation hook. Bleeding is controlled by compressing the wound with suction teeth introduced through the posterior port. Further dissection is performed to facilitate suturing. The surgeon uses a routine needle holder with a five-zero purling stitch through the anterior working port, first suturing one side of the wound and then the other, with the suction tip repositioned to expose the wound for knot tying.

During a thoracoscopic left upper lobectomy for adenocarcinoma, the surgeon transects the superior pulmonary vein and left upper lobe bronchus. Dense adhesions are observed around the pulmonary artery, which is carefully dissected to expose the lingual artery. An accidental laceration occurs at the junction of the lingual artery and main pulmonary artery, causing massive bleeding that stains the camera. The suction tip is used to compress the wound site for bleeding control, followed by the application of Alice forceps to clamp the vascular wound for further manipulation.

The surgeon dissects the left main pulmonary artery and secures it with an endoscopic atraumatic vascular clamp. This allows for careful inspection of the laceration. Angiography is performed using a running five-zero pulling stitch with an anastopic needle holder through the posterior working port, ensuring the contralateral wall of the artery is not inadvertently sewn.

The surgeon removes the vascular clamp and inspects for any leakage. The lingual artery is carefully dissected and transected, followed by the transection of the posterior ascending artery and the oblique fissure. The resected lobe is placed into a bag made from a rubber glove for removal.
